us.headline: Tariff stacks and origin rules — not mill prices — drive your landed cost.

regulation.b: China-origin goods carry additive Section 301 duties (many lists 25%; consumer List 4A 7.5%). USTR's second four-year review is underway — comment windows on the 2018 actions run through summer 2026 (the 23 Aug 2018 action window opens 24 Jun 2026) — and current product exclusions run only to 10 Nov 2026. Origin and HTS classification materially change landed cost; confirm each item's status before costing.
Duties compose additively (MFN + Section 301 + any Section 232 on steel/aluminium). Confirm each product's HTS code before costing.

us.supply: Asia–US (transpacific) spot rates reversed and are now spiking on early peak-season demand and pre-tariff frontloading (Shanghai–LA ≈ US$5,142/FEU, Shanghai–New York ≈ US$6,769/FEU in mid-June 2026) — booking space early matters more than chasing a dip. Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June 2026 — a near three-month low — as Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Strait-of-Hormuz traffic recovered, but US–Iran diplomacy stays unsettled and the lane remains volatile; resin and energy-linked input costs can swing quickly.
us.view: US landed cost is dominated by tariffs and origin, not spot material prices. Birdland's two-country production (Taiwan + China) lets you shift origin to manage tariff exposure, and we prepare clear origin documentation. With transpacific freight now firming into peak season, the smart move is to lock capacity and contract rates early — our pricing reflects secured capacity and compliant origin, not a single day's index.

ca.headline: ‘Country of melt’ now decides whether China steel surtaxes hit you.

regulation.b: Since 31 Jul 2025 Canada applies a 25% surtax on steel melted & poured (and aluminium cast) in China, on top of normal duties. From 26 Dec 2025 a 25% tariff also covers listed steel-derivative products — some with only partial steel content.

Several temporary remissions narrow from 1 Jul 2026 (relief ends for aluminium and for public-health/safety goods), though a Steel Derivative Goods Surtax Remission Order (Feb 2026; CBSA guidance Mar 2026) keeps category-based relief for a defined list. Confirm classification, country of melt and remission eligibility before shipping.

ca.supply: Landed steel cost into Canada is shaped more by origin-of-melt surtaxes than by mill prices. Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June 2026 — a near three-month low — as Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Strait-of-Hormuz traffic recovered, but US–Iran diplomacy stays unsettled and the lane remains volatile; resin and energy-linked input costs can swing quickly.
ca.view: Birdland can source Taiwan-melted steel content with clear traceability, helping you stay outside the China-melt surtax, and we document origin end-to-end. The driver here is provenance and paperwork, not spot steel — our quotes reflect verified origin and stable supply.

au.headline: Timber biosecurity and legal-logging compliance gate every wood-handled tool.

regulation.b: Wood-handled goods must meet DAFF biosecurity conditions — bark-free, pest-free, treated/certified — checked against the BICON database.
The Illegal Logging Prohibition Act requires due diligence on timber, and wood packaging must meet ISPM 15. Phytosanitary certificates have been electronic (ePhyto) since Aug 2025.

au.supply: Compliance and treatment readiness, more than freight price, determine clearance speed into Australia. Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June 2026 — a near three-month low — as Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Strait-of-Hormuz traffic recovered, but US–Iran diplomacy stays unsettled and the lane remains volatile; resin and energy-linked input costs can swing quickly.
au.view: Birdland supplies treated, certified, documented timber handles and ISPM 15-compliant packaging, so shipments clear cleanly. The value we sell is documented compliance and reliability — not the lowest spot timber cost.

uk.headline: Britain is diverging from the EU — plan to GB rules separately.

regulation.b: Great Britain sits outside the EU's EUDR and CBAM. The UK is developing its own forest-risk due-diligence rules, and a UK carbon border levy is planned (reportedly from 2027). Treat GB requirements separately from the EU.
UK product-marking and conformity rules still apply alongside EU rules for dual-market ranges — confirm marking per market.

uk.supply: Asia–Europe lanes are climbing steeply into the 2026 peak season (book early). Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June 2026 — a near three-month low — as Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Strait-of-Hormuz traffic recovered, but US–Iran diplomacy stays unsettled and the lane remains volatile; resin and energy-linked input costs can swing quickly.
uk.view: Selling into both the EU and GB means two rulebooks. Birdland prepares documentation for each destination and keeps origin traceable, so a dual-market range ships without surprises. Our pricing reflects this compliance work and secured supply.

me.headline: Energy and shipping volatility is the main planning risk — oil has eased but stays jumpy.

regulation.b: Gulf and wider Middle East markets use national/GCC conformity schemes (for example SASO-type approvals) that differ by country — confirm marking, certificates and labelling per destination.

me.supply: Strait-of-Hormuz flows recovered through June 2026 and Brent eased to around US$77/bbl after the US granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Hormuz traffic recovered, but the lane keeps fuel and freight volatile. Booking freight ahead buffers the swings.
me.view: In a volatile-energy region, predictability is the product. Birdland books freight early and holds key materials to cushion swings, and prepares per-market certificates. When oil eases, the right move is to lock rates — our quotes reflect secured supply, not a single day's price.

sa.headline: Duties, currency and documentation vary widely — plan per country.

regulation.b: Import duties and paperwork differ sharply across the region (Mercosur members vs others), and some markets apply anti-dumping measures. Confirm tariff classification and required certificates per country.

sa.supply: Currency volatility can move landed cost as much as freight or materials do. Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June 2026 — a near three-month low — as Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Strait-of-Hormuz traffic recovered, but US–Iran diplomacy stays unsettled and the lane remains volatile; resin and energy-linked input costs can swing quickly.
sa.view: Birdland plans shipments and pricing to cushion FX and duty swings, and provides clear origin documentation to speed customs. The value is steady, well-documented supply — not chasing the cheapest spot input.

global.headline: Three forces shape 2026: tighter EU compliance, tariff/origin rules, and freight + energy volatility.

regulation.b: The EU Deforestation Regulation applies from 30 Dec 2026 for large/medium operators (small & micro to 30 Jun 2027). Wood-handled tools need geolocation data and proof of legal, deforestation-free timber. In May 2026 the Commission issued a simplification package (revised guidance, FAQs and a draft scope act) and is relaunching the information system over summer 2026, but the core obligations stand — prepare documentation now.

regulation.t: CBAM — carbon cost on steel content
regulation.b: CBAM entered its definitive phase on 1 Jan 2026; financial obligations on embedded carbon in imported steel now apply, with the first certificate surrender in 2027. On 12 Jun 2026 the Council agreed its position to strengthen CBAM — extending to selected downstream products and adding anti-circumvention rules. Steel-content items (blades, frames) face added landed cost and reporting.

US Section 301 duties stack additively and current exclusions run only to 10 Nov 2026 (with USTR weighing further tariff actions), while Canada's 25% surtax on China-melted steel means country of origin increasingly decides landed cost.

global.supply: Asia–Europe and transpacific freight are both firming sharply into peak season (Drewry WCI ≈ US$3,969, +12% w/w 18 Jun 2026); Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June after a US 60-day Iran oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Hormuz recovery, though it stays volatile on US–Iran uncertainty; resin and energy-linked input costs can swing quickly.
global.view: Across every market the pattern is the same: compliance documentation and origin now matter more than spot prices. Birdland's two-country production, early freight booking, material locking and full compliance paperwork (EUDR, CBAM data, ISPM 15, origin) keep you a season ahead. Our quotes reflect secured materials, compliant documentation and reliable supply — when an index dips, that stability is exactly what protects your margin.

nl.headline: EU rules tighten on timber, steel carbon and chemicals — documentation is now the deciding factor.

regulation.b: The EU Deforestation Regulation applies from 30 Dec 2026 for large/medium operators (small & micro to 30 Jun 2027). Wood-handled tools need geolocation data and proof of legal, deforestation-free timber. In May 2026 the Commission issued a simplification package (revised guidance, FAQs and a draft scope act) and is relaunching the information system over summer 2026, but the core obligations stand — prepare documentation now.

regulation.t: CBAM — carbon cost on steel content
regulation.b: CBAM entered its definitive phase on 1 Jan 2026; financial obligations on embedded carbon in imported steel now apply, with the first certificate surrender in 2027. On 12 Jun 2026 the Council agreed its position to strengthen CBAM — extending to selected downstream products and adding anti-circumvention rules. Steel-content items (blades, frames) face added landed cost and reporting.

ECHA's final PFAS consultation closed 25 May 2026; SEAC's final opinion is due end-2026 and adoption is expected in 2027. May affect fluorinated coatings and finishes — review handle/blade coatings early.

nl.supply: Asia–Europe ocean freight is climbing steeply into the 2026 peak season (Shanghai–Rotterdam ≈ US$4,342/FEU, +15% w/w in mid-June; Drewry WCI ≈ US$3,969 on 18 Jun), while Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June — a near three-month low — after Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Hormuz traffic recovered, though the lane stays volatile. CBAM carbon charges lift steel landed costs regardless of mill prices.
nl.view: As an EU destination, the Netherlands rewards suppliers who arrive compliance-ready. Birdland prepares EUDR geolocation and legal-timber evidence, supports CBAM carbon data on steel parts, and reviews coatings against PFAS — while booking freight early and locking key materials. When costs dip, the honest takeaway is not “cut price” but “lock stability before the next swing”: our quotes reflect secured materials, compliant documentation and reliable supply, not yesterday's spot price.

eu.headline: EU rules tighten on timber, steel carbon and chemicals — documentation is now the deciding factor.

regulation.b: The EU Deforestation Regulation applies from 30 Dec 2026 for large/medium operators (small & micro to 30 Jun 2027). Wood-handled tools need geolocation data and proof of legal, deforestation-free timber. In May 2026 the Commission issued a simplification package (revised guidance, FAQs and a draft scope act) and is relaunching the information system over summer 2026, the no-enforcement grace period for in-scope operators ends 30 Jun 2026, but the core obligations stand — prepare documentation now.

regulation.t: CBAM — carbon cost on steel content
regulation.b: CBAM entered its definitive phase on 1 Jan 2026; financial obligations on embedded carbon in imported steel now apply, with the first certificate surrender in 2027. On 12 Jun 2026 the Council agreed its position to strengthen CBAM — extending to selected downstream products and adding anti-circumvention rules. eu.supply: Asia–Europe ocean freight is climbing steeply into the 2026 peak season (Shanghai–Rotterdam ≈ US$4,342/FEU, +15% w/w in mid-June; Drewry WCI ≈ US$3,969 on 18 Jun), while Brent crude slipped to around US$77/bbl by late June — a near three-month low — after Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Hormuz traffic recovered, though the lane stays volatile. CBAM carbon charges lift steel landed costs regardless of mill prices.
eu.view: As an EU destination, Europe rewards suppliers who arrive compliance-ready. Birdland prepares EUDR geolocation and legal-timber evidence, supports CBAM carbon data on steel parts, and reviews coatings against PFAS — while booking freight early and locking key materials. When costs dip, the honest takeaway is not “cut price” but “lock stability before the next swing”: our quotes reflect secured materials, compliant documentation and reliable supply, not yesterday's spot price.

teamAnalysis: Compliance and origin — not spot prices — are setting landed cost into 2026. EU buyers face EUDR, CBAM and PFAS tightening together; US and Canadian cost is driven by tariff stacks and country-of-origin/melt rules. Ocean freight has turned sharply upward on both Asia–Europe and transpacific lanes as peak season and pre-tariff frontloading collide, while Brent crude eased to around US$80 as Strait-of-Hormuz flows recovered — though energy stays volatile on US–Iran uncertainty. Read the moves as reasons to lock supply, capacity and documentation early, not to chase the oil dip: Birdland's two-country production, early freight booking, material locking and full compliance paperwork keep your shelves stocked and your margin steady when these indices swing.

news.title: Birdland at spoga+gafa 2026 — Hall 04.2 / C011, Cologne, 22–24 June
news.blurb: Our team is exhibiting at spoga+gafa 2026 (Koelnmesse, Cologne, 22–24 June 2026) — find us at Hall 04.2, Stand C011. Cologne is expected warm and mostly dry those days (around 24–27°C by day, light winds). To arrange a meeting, please contact our representatives Nancy or Jason. We warmly welcome partners to visit our booth.

news.title: EUDR readiness: how we document timber for EU buyers
news.blurb: What geolocation and legal-timber evidence we prepare so wood-handled lines clear from 30 Dec 2026.

news.title: Two countries, one standard: how we keep supply moving
news.blurb: A look at the Taiwan + China model that lets orders shift when a route or tariff is hit.

news.title: New for the season: the Fieldwork hand-tool range
news.blurb: Fruit pickers, grape knives, seeders, leaf rakes, litter pickers and long-reach pruners.

forward.note: Forward curves are indicative reads for procurement timing, not tradable quotes.

commodities.name: Steel HRC
commodities.tip: Upward near-term on CBAM carbon load & demand; easing by 12m. Locking 3–6m looks favourable.

commodities.name: Brent Crude
commodities.tip: Eased from June highs as Hormuz flows recovered; backwardation persists — low urgency to pre-buy fuel-linked inputs.

commodities.name: PP Resin
commodities.tip: Mild contango — forward priced higher; consider locking resin for handles now.

commodities.name: Container Freight
commodities.tip: Peak-season surge now (both Asia–EU and transpacific); eases H2. Book peak space early.

landed.note: Spot raw materials are a minority of landed cost; conversion, freight, duty & service dominate — which is why a dip in any one index moves your price far less than the headline suggests.

components.name: Steel & metal parts
components.tip: Blade & mechanism steel — exposed to HRC & scrap.

components.name: Labour & assembly
components.tip: Conversion, QC, calibration — your stable, non-spot cost.

items.label: CBAM definitive phase
items.tip: Carbon obligations on embedded steel begin.

tariff_calc.note: Illustrative for garden hand-tools; confirm your exact HS code & origin with your broker.

addons.label: Section 301 (China origin)
addons.tip: China-origin only; Taiwan origin avoids it.

addons.label: Surtax — China-melted steel
addons.tip: Chinese country-of-melt; Taiwan melt avoids it.

addons.label: CBAM carbon cost (steel)
addons.tip: Not a tariff — a separate carbon charge.

procurement.note: Desk recommendations on what to lock now vs wait, from forward curves and policy.

items.why: Curve rising near-term (CBAM + demand); eases by 12m.
Both Asia–Europe and transpacific spiking into peak season; eases H2.
Mild contango — forward priced above spot.
Brent eased to ~US$80 on Hormuz recovery; backwardation — stay spot.
EUDR 30 Dec 2026; CBAM live — documentation is the gate.

material.note: Metal & polymer outlook for your bill of materials. Curves below; lock guidance in the Procurement Brief.

war.note: Geopolitical supply-chain risk monitor — logistics & cost impact only.

zones.note: Diversions lengthen Asia–Europe transit and lift rates.
Flows recovered through June; Brent eased to ~US$77 after a US 60-day Iran oil-sales licence (22 Jun) and Hormuz recovery, but it stays volatile.
Energy and input-cost spillover into EU.
Monitored; Birdland's two-country base is a hedge.

procurement.summary: Compliance and origin are the swing factors into 2026, not spot prices. Lock steel and peak freight early, keep US-bound volume on Taiwan origin, and get EUDR timber documentation ready now. The desk's priority actions are below.

actions.label: Lock 3–6M steel (HRC)
actions.note: Curve in contango + CBAM load — secure now.

actions.label: Book peak Asia–EU space
actions.note: Both Asia–EU and transpacific spiking into peak; reserve early.

actions.label: Keep US orders on Taiwan origin

shipping.note: Indicative planning guide. Solid = last 3 months; dotted = next 6 months (US$/FEU).

material.note: The 8 raw materials garden-tool buyers care about most, rebased to 100 = now. Solid = last 3 months; dotted = next 6 months. Hover a line to highlight it.

war.note: Geopolitical impact on your supply chain (0–100). Birdland's two-country base buffers single-point shocks.

items.note: Brent ~US$105; tanker-risk premia feed fuel & freight.
Re-routing round Africa adds days & cost.
Watch item; dual Taiwan+China base mitigates.

p-brief.simple: Think of this like a weather forecast for your wallet — grab the umbrella before it rains.
expert: The brief ranks actions by the cost of waiting: lock steel and peak-season ocean space, and keep US-bound volume on Taiwan origin to dodge tariff stacking.
Each item is prioritised so procurement can sequence commitments against budget and cash flow.
Prepare EUDR timber documentation in parallel — 30 Dec 2026 is a hard gate for EU-bound wooden-handle lines.

p-material.simple: Like checking if your recipe's ingredients are about to get pricier, so you stock the pantry early.
expert: Steel (HRC) carries near-term upward pressure from CBAM carbon costs and demand, easing only later.
Polypropylene resin shows mild contango — forward above spot — favouring partial early locks for handles.
Rebasing to 100 = now compares momentum across materials, not just absolute prices.

p-shipping.simple: Like booking flights before the holiday rush — wait too long and the container seat costs far more.
expert: Asia–North Europe rates firm into the 2026 peak before easing in H2, so booking peak space early caps cost.
Transpacific lanes are softer — a window to lock favourable US contract rates.
Lane-level timing matters more for your delivered cost than the headline index.

p-freight.simple: These are the speedometers of shipping — bulk (BDI) and container (FEU) show how fast freight costs move.
expert: FRED's deep-sea PPI and Cass index are official, free monthly readings of freight cost and volume.
BDI signals bulk/dry demand; container FEU rates drive your boxed-goods landed cost.
Monthly series lag 1–2 months — use the official source links for the latest spot figures.

p-calc.simple: A kitchen scale for your total cost — turn the dials and watch steel, freight and duty change the price per tool.
expert: Raw materials are a minority of landed cost; conversion, freight and duty dominate — so one index dipping moves your price far less than the headline.
Changing duty and origin shows how country-of-origin choices change the bottom line.
Per-unit and order-total outputs translate market moves into real purchase-order impact.

p-tariff.simple: A traffic-light board for import taxes — red means a new toll just appeared on your route.
expert: It tracks recent and pending duty changes by market, including US Section 301 proposals and Canada's steel surtax.
Country of origin and country-of-melt increasingly decide whether a surtax applies.
Pair it with the landed-cost calculator to quantify each change before it hits a costing.

p-war.simple: A storm-tracker for your supply chain — it shows which conflicts could blow shipments off course.
expert: Each event is scored 0–100 for pressure on oil, freight, transit time and insurance.
Strait-of-Hormuz and Red Sea risks feed fuel and freight volatility most directly.
Birdland's two-country production buffers single-point shocks, keeping supply moving if one route is hit.

p-mkt.simple: Like reading two top weather stations (BNP Paribas & Citi), then telling you what to pack for your market.
expert: Cross-referencing publicly reported BNP Paribas and Citi research: Citi sees Brent crude easing toward ~US$60/bbl into late 2026 — a tailwind for fuel- and freight-linked costs.
BNP Paribas flags tariff-reconfiguration risk (the US tariff court ruling, USMCA renegotiation) and China–EU trade tensions as the key watch-items.
Birdland's takeaway for your sourcing: treat softer energy as a planning cushion, not a reason to chase spot — lock supply and keep origin flexible while tariff rules are in flux.
p-mkt.src: Based on publicly reported views (group.bnpparibas, citigroup.com) — interpreted by the Birdland team, not the banks' own advice.

p-news.simple: The company noticeboard — the latest from the Birdland team, including where to meet us.
expert: Posts are curated by Birdland and never auto-generated, so they reflect real company updates.
The newest item appears first; click through for details and links.
Trade-show notices (e.g. spoga+gafa) include stand and contact details for meetings.

freight.note: Freight-market signals. BDI = bulk/dry shipping; container lanes = US$/FEU. Indicative until a live source is connected.

items.label: Ocean freight cost (US PPI · deep-sea)
items.tip: US producer-price index for deep-sea freight transportation (monthly, FRED) — a free, reliable ocean-freight-cost trend.

items.label: Cass Freight Index · shipments
items.tip: US freight shipment volumes (monthly, FRED) — broad freight-demand signal.

items.label: Baltic Dry Index
items.tip: Baltic Dry Index — cost of moving bulk/dry cargo (Capesize/Panamax/Supramax). A macro shipping-demand signal; relevant to bulk-shipped orders.

items.label: Container · Asia → N. Europe
items.tip: Spot container rate Asia to North Europe (per 40ft). Your containerised lane to EU.

items.label: Container · Asia → US West
items.tip: Spot container rate Asia to US West Coast (per 40ft).

items.label: Container · Asia → US East
items.tip: Spot container rate Asia to US East Coast (per 40ft).

c-desk.simple: This is the desk's one-line 'what it all means' — like a teacher summing up the whole chapter in a sentence.
expert: It distils regulation, tariffs and freight into the few moves that actually change your landed cost.
Read it first, then drill into the panels below for the detail behind each point.
AI-assisted and refreshed regularly — framed as Birdland's view, not advice.

c-watch.simple: A scoreboard for your raw materials — green up, red down, like a stock ticker for your cost inputs.
expert: Each row is an input (oil, steel, resin, freight, tariff pressure) with its latest level, change and trend.
Hover a row for what that move means for your landed cost.
Use it as the at-a-glance pulse before reading the deeper panels.

c-freight.simple: These are the speedometers of shipping — bulk (BDI) and container (FEU) show how fast freight costs move.
expert: FRED's deep-sea PPI and Cass index are official, free monthly readings of freight cost and volume.
BDI signals bulk/dry demand; container FEU rates drive your boxed-goods landed cost.
Monthly series lag 1–2 months — use the official source links for the latest spot figures.

c-curve.simple: Like a tide chart — it tells you if prices are heading up (lock now) or down (wait).
expert: A rising curve (contango) means forward prices are higher, so locking now can pay off.
A falling curve (backwardation) means the market expects easing — less urgency.
Steel, oil, resin and freight each give their own procurement-timing signal.

c-cost.simple: Shows what really fills your cost pie — raw material is just one slice, not the whole pie.
expert: Conversion, freight, duty and service dominate landed cost, so a dip in one index moves your price far less than the headline.
That's why Birdland's quotes reflect secured materials and reliable supply, not yesterday's spot price.
Hover a segment to see each component's share.

c-duty.simple: A toll calculator for imports — pick the country and the origin, and it shows the import tax on your route.
expert: Switching origin (Taiwan vs China) shows how much tariff stacking you avoid.
Figures are illustrative for garden hand-tools — confirm your HS code with your broker.
Pair it with the cost breakdown to see duty's real weight.

c-mkt.simple: Like reading two top weather stations (BNP Paribas & Citi), then telling you what to pack for your market.
expert: Cross-referencing publicly reported BNP Paribas and Citi research: Citi sees Brent crude easing toward ~US$60/bbl into late 2026 — a tailwind for fuel- and freight-linked costs.
BNP Paribas flags tariff-reconfiguration risk (the US tariff court ruling, USMCA renegotiation) and China–EU trade tensions as the key watch-items.
Birdland's takeaway for your sourcing: treat softer energy as a planning cushion, not a reason to chase spot — lock supply and keep origin flexible while tariff rules are in flux.
c-mkt.src: Based on publicly reported views (group.bnpparibas, citigroup.com) — interpreted by the Birdland team, not the banks' own advice.

usdtwd_view.text: Near-term USD/TWD leans to mild TWD depreciation: a still-patient Fed and lingering US–China trade friction support the dollar, while Taiwan's central bank stays steady. Watch a 31.9–32.7 range over the next 1–4 weeks; exporters may phase in forward cover rather than wait.

advice.zh: 【Birdland 供應鏈週報】本週亞洲—歐洲海運進入旺季、運價走升,亞洲—美西回軟;鋼價偏強(CBAM 碳成本),木材與塑膠粒走軟。建議:歐洲線提前訂艙鎖運費、鋼質料件分批提前鎖料;美線維持彈性、可待現貨。法規面 EUDR(2026/12/30)木柄文件請提早備齊。需要我們協助規劃備料節奏與文件,隨時聯繫。
advice.en: [Birdland Supply-Chain Brief] Asia–Europe ocean freight is firming into peak season while Asia–US West Coast eases; steel stays firm on CBAM carbon costs, with lumber and resin softening. We suggest booking Europe-bound space early to lock freight and phasing in steel-input cover, while staying flexible on US lanes. On compliance, prepare EUDR (30 Dec 2026) timber documentation early. Contact us to plan your buying cadence and paperwork.

materials_bot.simple: 原料像做菜的食材——鋼和包材在漲、木材和塑膠在跌,整體成本『一半漲一半跌』,不會單看一項就決定報價。
expert: 對台灣出口:鋼與電鍍/包材偏漲,鋼質手工具到岸成本壓力升,建議分批鎖鋼料、優化包材規格。
對中國出口:木材與樹膠走軟有利木柄/握把成本,但 EUDR(木材)合規文件成關鍵門檻。
綜合:成本結構分散,維持台灣+中國雙基地調度,用『鎖料+文件齊備』把波動轉成穩定供應的賣點。

shipping.name: 海運運價 亞洲→歐洲(FEU)
shipping.note: 旺季走升,提前訂艙鎖價。

shipping.name: 海運運價 亞洲→美西(FEU)
港口雜費 THC/港建費
shipping.note: 兩岸港口費率穩定,留意旺季附加。

shipping.name: 國內拖車/內陸運輸
shipping.note: 柴油與缺工推升,工廠到港拖運緩漲。

shipping.name: 海關倉儲/查驗費
shipping.note: 查驗趨嚴、滯港風險升,倉儲成本走高。

shipping.name: 旺季附加費 PSS/GRI
shipping.note: 歐洲線旺季可能加徵,先估入成本。

shipping_bot.simple: 海運成本像搭車的車資——歐洲線塞車漲價、美西順暢降價;港口和拖車的『小費』也在變。
expert: 對台灣/中國出口:歐洲線運價與旺季附加偏漲,建議提前訂艙、鎖長約;美西回軟可保留彈性。
內陸拖運與海關倉儲/查驗成本上升,出貨節奏與文件齊備能直接省下滯港與查驗成本。
綜合:把運費波動納入報價(BAF/PSS 先估),用提前佈局把不確定變可預期,是對客戶最有價值的服務。

reg_bot.simple: 法規像考試規則一直在改——歐美越來越看『木材哪來的、鋼的碳排多少、有沒有強迫勞動』,文件不齊就卡關。
expert: 對台灣出口:CBAM(鋼)、EUDR(木)文件成歐洲市場門檻;台灣熔煉鋼還能規避加拿大附加稅,是優勢。
對中國出口:原產地查驗趨嚴 + 美 UFLPA,『中國製』暴露於關稅與審查,雙基地調度與產地文件是關鍵。
綜合:把『合規文件齊備』做成標準服務,等於替客戶清掉通關風險,是最強的差異化。
